text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 12 September 2025 

Dr Sarah Ahmed 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
St Thomas’ Hospital, London SE1 7EH  

Dear Dr Sarah Ahmed, 

Alex Morgan, DOB: 22-03-1992, Hospital No: L9483721 NHS No. 1038475629  
Flat 4, 29 Riverside Walk, London, SE16 5DN  

Diagnosis:

Combined generalised and focal epilepsy: history indicates both generalised seizure phenomena earlier in the course and ongoing focal-onset events presently; prior records document generalised spike–wave activity alongside focal temporal discharges on EEG.

Aetiology:

Unclear aetiology; perinatal and developmental history unremarkable. No structural lesion identified on prior MRI (2023). Family history negative for epilepsy. 

Other Medical and Social Background:

Post-partum within the last six months; night-time sleep markedly fragmented due to infant care. Reports increased fatigue and irregular meal patterns. No alcohol or recreational drug use. Breastfeeding established; has discussed medication compatibility with perinatal team.

Seizure Description and Current Control:

He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically 3 - 4 episodes daily. These are occasionally accompanied by brief disorientation but resolve spontaneously. No generalised tonic-clonic seizures have occurred since last year. Caregivers feel overall control is acceptable. These focal events are most frequent in the late evening and during periods of pronounced sleep disruption. There have been no injuries, tongue biting, or incontinence reported with recent episodes.

Treatment History and Response:

- Levetiracetam previously trialled: partial reduction in generalised events but provoked mood irritability; discontinued in 2024.
- Lamotrigine currently continued: moderate benefit for focal seizures without adverse effects; serum levels within therapeutic range at last check.
- Valproate avoided given post-partum and reproductive considerations previously discussed pre-conception.

Current Medicines:

Lamotrigine 150 mg mane and 200 mg nocte (single brand). Folic acid 400 micrograms daily. Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily.

Management Plan:

- Continue Lamotrigine at the current doses and single brand, given stability in generalised seizure freedom since last year and acceptable focal seizure burden per patient and caregivers.
- Arrange ambulatory EEG to further characterise the focal events and screen for interictal generalised activity.
- Safety advice reinforced, with emphasis on nocturnal supervision technologies and avoiding lone bathing; driving not applicable at present given ongoing focal impaired-awareness episodes.
- Sleep optimisation: patient reports fragmented night-time sleep related to infant care; discussed non-prescriptive strategies such as brief scheduled daytime rest when support is available, consistent sleep–wake cues, and sharing night feeds where feasible.
- Review in three months, earlier if any generalised tonic–clonic seizure recurs, seizures cluster, or injuries occur. Will recheck Lamotrigine level at that visit.

Summary:

This patient has combined generalised and focal epilepsy, with prior generalised seizures now in remission over the past year and persistent daily focal impaired-awareness events occurring 3–4 times per day. Post-partum sleep fragmentation appears to correlate with event frequency. Ongoing management will focus on maintaining generalised seizure freedom, characterising residual focal activity, and addressing modifiable lifestyle contributors.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically 3 - 4 episodes daily. These are occasionally accompanied by brief disorientation but resolve spontaneously. No generalised tonic-clonic seizures have occurred since last year. Caregivers feel overall control is acceptable

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient has ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically 3 - 4 episodes daily. It also notes no generalized tonic-clonic seizures since last year, but the highest current frequency is the focal events at 3–4 per day. Convert this to the allowed format using 'to' for ranges and 'per day'.
reference: He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically 3 - 4 episodes daily. These are occasionally accompanied by brief disorientation but resolve spontaneously. No generalised tonic-clonic seizures have occurred since last year. Caregivers feel overall control is acceptable
